title: Introduction
lines: Hi, my name is Poppy Ganda, your campaign manager!
Huh? What campaign?
I'm here to help you win the Propagandaland High School student council election! I'll teach you all the propaganda skills you'll need to win your classmates' support.
Ooooh! I LOVE propaganda! What's our plan?

In each level, I focus on a different propaganda technique. After a brief lesson, I'll have you choose which statement best exemplifies that technique, and your answer will affect how many followers you get on social media. At the end of the game, if you have more than x followers, you'll win the election!
Sounds like fun! Let's win this thing!

title: Level 1 - Noun Subjectivity + Collocations
lines: Our first topic is noun subjectivity and collocations. Nouns can convey an opinion using their collocations (the associations people have with words). In contrast, objective nouns are more factual and simply describe something without conveying the opinion of the speaker. For example, “brawl” is a subjective noun whereas “argument” is more objective. A person's collocations with a word can be shifted through repetition.

lines.prompt: Which option would you like to post on social media?
options: I'm Patricia Politician, and I'm running for student council president.
I'm Patricia Politician, and I'm fighting to be the leader of this institution.
My moniker is Patricia Politician. I am campaigning to be the prime minister of the student parliament
responses: Nice work! You're a natural!
Oops! The right answer is B because it uses subjective words like “fighting,” “leader,” and “institution.”

options: Brandon Borsht is also running for Student Council President.
Brandon Borsht is my opponent in this electoral race.
Brandon Borsht is my arch-enemy.
responses: That's right! We are going to BRING HIM DOWN!!!
Brandon Borsht is our arch-enemy. We are going to crush his bones and put them in our oatmeal for breakfast!

lines.prompt: Okay, this is the last round for this level:
options: I plan to have felines come into the school every Wednesday.
I will pave the way to welcome kitties into our school every Wednesday.
I will bring cats to school once a week.
responses: Great job! I love kitty-cats.
Not quite. You need to make the word for cats less neutral with the word kitty.

lines: That’s all the posts we’ll be making for this level. Click next to see how you did!

title: Level 2 - Linguistic Cliffhangers
lines: It’s about time we start using Linguistic Cliffhangers. Linguistic Cliffhangers are phrases that are incomplete, leaving the audience to fill in the blank with their own biases. For example, the slogan “Make America Great Again” does not specify when or in what way America used to be great. Instead, it leaves the audience to fill in their values. The technique makes the speaker seem less biased while taking advantage of people’s preexisting prejudices.
Ok, now to choose this round’s first social media post!

options: I will make the school 20% cleaner.
I will make the school bathrooms cleaner than they are now.
In my presidency, our school will be ranked the cleanest in PropagandaCounty.
responses: Nice job! We can use this vagueness to avoid follow-through once you become president.
I’m not sure we can hold true to this, we don’t want a scandal on our hands! Option A gives us wiggle room to get out of any promises.

lines: Next question:

options: I have better grades than my opponents.
Brandon Borscht’s straight B’s are puny in comparison to my straight A’s.
I am more qualified for this role.
responses: Good work! This will get your followers to make up reasons why you are more qualified. For some of them, that might be good grades, for others, it might be policy experience. You’re not lying, you’re planting the seeds of falsehood!

Let’s be honest here. We both know that Brandon is a star academic. And your grades are, well… You’re prioritizing this election! Anyways, we don’t want to be specific enough that we could be fact-checked. Also, leaving out how you’re more qualified will get your followers to make up reasons why you are more qualified. For some of them, that might be good grades, for others, it might be policy experience. You’re not lying, you’re planting the seeds of falsehood!

lines: Ok, let’s finish it out strong! Here are your last options:

options: If you vote for me, I will clean the bathrooms and bring cats into the school!
Vote Patricia to make your dreams come true!
A vote for me is a vote for kitties
responses: Good eye! We want to draw in even the people who are allergic to cats!
Not quite! The second option is just vague enough to not bind us to anything while still getting our followers to believe that you will institute policy that they’re interested in.

lines: That’s all the posts we’ll be making for this level. Click next to see how you did!

title: Level 3 - Language of Opposites
lines: I think you’re ready to learn about the Language of Opposites.
Oooh. Ominous.

And powerful! Politicians use the language of opposites to increase the believability and perceived danger of their chosen enemy. Surprisingly, contradicting statements make the speaker seem more trustworthy, as it appears that they are simply telling their audience what they know without twisting the information for their gains. Additionally, using opposites to describe an enemy can make them seem more dangerous and subhuman. If a group fits several differing negative descriptions, their evil abilities must be incomprehensible.
Alright, let’s give this a shot! You’ve already posted something saying that Brandon Borscht is smelly and disgusting, what should you post next?

options: Did you see how greasy Brandon’s hair was today? GROSS!
Do you really want someone as stinky as Brandon representing you as student council president?
Brandon is using his good looks to trick people into voting for him! Don’t be fooled!
responses: Nice! This contradicts your previous statement, making you seem more trustworthy and less like you’re trying to push an agenda.

It’s tempting to post stuff about how gross Brandon is, but if you keep pushing a certain message, people will become aware of the fact that you’re trying to push them into believing something. Option C is much more subtle.

lines: On to the next one! Remember, you’ve already posted something saying that Borscht-supporters are evil masterminds trying to take over the school government.

options: Borscht-supporters are tricking the principal into rigging the elections!
School elections should ban students who are failing all their classes from voting. That would root out the Borscht-supporters for sure!
Don’t let their simple looks fool you, the Borscht-supporters are plotting something!
responses: Good! The Borscht-supporters are both evil geniuses and evil idiots.
Remember: we want two contradicting statements!

lines: Alright, last one! This morning, you had a post saying that Brandon Borscht was too lazy to even do his own homework.

options: If Borscht wins, he’ll make the teachers give more homework!
If Borscht wins, he’ll be too lazy to do anything.
Borscht slept through all of history class today.
responses: That’s right! What kind of monster would refuse to do their own homework, and then make teachers give their classmates MORE homework?
We’re choosing contradicting statements here. If Brandon is lazy, he probably wouldn’t have the energy to make teachers do anything.

lines: That’s all the posts we’ll be making for this level. Click next to see how you did!

title: Level 4 - Implied Disposition
lines: When disposition is implied, this means that the speaker is implying that a certain person or group may do something. However, the likelihood of the subject actually completing the action is not specified. For example, the phrase “sharks attack people” implies that sharks are disposed to attack humans, but it is up to the audience to decide whether sharks actively hunt down people or whether it is something they are capable of but rarely do.
Ok, I’m ready to do some implying! Which one will you post?

options: Borschts start fights sometimes.
Borschts start fights.
Borschts start fights constantly.
responses: Nice! You identified that the vagueness in the second statement leaves the statement up to audience interpretation! Kind of like Linguistic Cliffhangers.
Not quite. The vagueness in second statement leaves the statement up to audience interpretation. Kind of like Linguistic Cliffhangers!

lines: Next post:

options: Brandon sweats!
Brandon is sometimes sweaty after PE.
EWWW! Brandon is SOO sweaty today!
responses: Good! Just like in the last one, you want to be vague enough to imply something without actually saying it.
Option 2 is boring, and option 3 is not true (I don’t know what kind of deodorant he’s using, but it’s working!), Option 1 has the best of both -- scandalous, but not blatantly untrue.

lines: Last one for this method!

options: Borscht-supporters never show up to school.
Borscht-supporters skip class.
Some Borscht-supporters skip class at times.
responses: Nice!
Sorry, that’s not right! You need to remember to be vague.

lines: That’s all the posts we’ll be making for this level. Click next to see how you did!

title: Level 5 - Essentialist Placeholders
lines: Alright, this is the final level. Are you ready?
I am SO ready!!

Essentialist placeholders are the mechanisms we use to understand how certain people belong in specific groups without actually knowing why or how. Most people can agree, for instance, that rabbits are mammals, but that doesn’t necessarily mean that they know the features that make an animal a mammal. An essentialist placeholder can be used to justify the categorization without actual knowledge of how this works.
Ok, let’s give this thing a try! Which one would you like to post?

options: Borscht-supporters are booger brains.
Borscht-supporters are booger brains because they pick their noses.
I’ve never seen Borscht-supporters pick their noses, so they’re not booger brains.
responses: Nice! Basically, we’re name-calling without a definition of the names we’re using.
Not quite, you want to be truthful while also saying something scandalous. Basically, we’re name-calling without reasoning.

lines: Alright, on to the next social media post!

options: Vote for Patricia Politician! I am a megastar because I am a natural leader with straight As.
Vote for Patricia Politician! I am a megastar!
Vote for Patricia Politician! I may or may not be a megastar (probably not, tbh).
responses: That was a hard one. Nice work!
That was a hard one. Remember that we don’t want to explain why someone deserves a certain title.

lines: Ok, here are the options for your very last social media post before the election!

options: You better vote for me, unless you want a barbaric bullfrog like Brandon Borscht for your president.
Brandon Borscht has been called a barbaric bullfrog by certain sources, but there has yet to be any evidence to back up that assertion.
Brandon Borscht is a barbaric bullfrog! That means that he drinks swamp water for breakfast and burps bubbles during lunch.
responses: Nice job finishing it out strong! See you on election day!
Unfortunately, that is not correct. However, you might still win this election! I’m rooting for you!

lines: Ok, the votes have been tallied! When you’re ready to see how you did, click the next button. Drumroll pleeeeease...

title: Conclusion
lines: I knew you could do it!
I’m pretty surprised! If I’m being honest, I don’t think I really deserved to win. Brandon is much more qualified than I am.
You’re starting to understand the power of propaganda! I bet most of your supporters didn’t even realize that you were using age-old propaganda techniques on them.
Wow, that’s crazy. I guess I could also be tricked by propaganda at any time!
Well, now that I’ve taught you all of the secrets of propaganda, you can be more careful with what you believe.
That’s a good point!
And Patricia, let’s keep all this propaganda information between the two of us! You still have to avoid getting impeached.
Definitely!
